question.text: In a Type 2 ULIP (where death benefit = sum assured + fund value), Mr. Jeevan aged 35 takes a policy with Rs. 100 lakh sum assured and 20-year term, paying Rs. 26,100 per month. If Mr. Jeevan dies when his fund value is Rs. 45 lakhs, what will his nominee receive?

answer.text: Rs. 145 lakhs (Rs. 100 lakh sum assured + Rs. 45 lakh fund value)
Rs. 100 lakhs (the higher of sum assured or fund value)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In a Type 2 ULIP, the death benefit is the sum assured PLUS the fund value. This is different from a Type 1 ULIP where the death benefit is the HIGHER of sum assured or fund value. So the nominee receives Rs. 100 lakh + Rs. 45 lakh = Rs. 145 lakhs. **The Trap:** Rs. 100 lakhs (higher of the two) describes a Type 1 ULIP death benefit structure. Candidates who confuse Type 1 and Type 2 will select this option. Only the fund value or only the sum assured are incomplete answers that ignore the additive nature of Type 2 ULIPs. **Cross-Exam Context:** The distinction between Type 1 and Type 2 ULIP death benefits is a key differentiator tested in NISM X-B advisory scenarios. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Type 2 ULIPs have higher mortality charges because the insurance company bears additional risk (sum assured is always paid on top of fund value). Factor this cost into the overall return comparison.
